title: High-throughput sequencing SELEX for determining DNA-binding protein specificities in vitro
author: Pantier, Chhatbar, et al.

steps.title: Before you begin
steps.instruction: Systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment (SELEX) is a molecular biology technique allowing the in vitro selection of DNA oligonucleotide duplexes with high affinity for a target ligand (Ellington and Szostak, 1990; Tuerk and Gold, 1990). This technology can be coupled with high-throughput sequencing (HT-SELEX) to determine transcription factor binding specificities (Roulet etÂ al., 2002; Jolma etÂ al., 2010; Slattery etÂ al., 2011).

steps.title: Before you begin
steps.instruction: Here, we describe the stepwise performance and analysis of HT-SELEX using purified SALL4 C2H2 zinc-finger clusters as âbaitâ (Pantier etÂ al., 2021). However, this protocol can be applied to a wide range of DNA-binding proteins or DNA-binding domains (see limitations). Two critical reagents are required to initiate HT-SELEX: a library of random oligonucleotides; and a purified DNA-binding protein fused with an affinity tag.

steps.title: Perform SELEX (repeat these steps 2â6 times)
steps.instruction: During SELEX, a library of random oligonucleotides is mixed with a DNA-binding protein of interest fused with an affinity tag. Protein-DNA complexes are purified and bound sequences are amplified by the polymerase chain reaction (PCR). This material is re-used for successive cycles of SELEX until most of the library contains high affinity binding sites. For transcription factors, 2â3 cycles are usually sufficient for successful HT-SELEX (Jolma etÂ al., 2010, 2013). However, we performed up to 6Ã SELEX cycles to characterize SALL4 ZFC4 which promiscuously binds to multiple AT-rich sequences (Pantier etÂ al., 2021).1.Prepare buffers.

steps.title: Perform SELEX (repeat these steps 2â6 times)
steps.instruction: On the day of the experiment, prepare a mastermix of âSELEX binding bufferâ (SELEX buffer supplemented with 5Â Îỳg/mL poly(dI-dC) and 0.5Â mM DTT) and âSELEX wash bufferâ (SELEX buffer supplemented with 0.5Â mM DTT).SELEX binding buffer (for up to 500Â ÎỳL of Ni Sepharose 6 Fast Flow resin)ReagentFinal concentrationAmountSELEX Buffern/a5Â mL1Â M DTT0.5Â mM2.5Â ÎỳL1Â mg/mL poly(dI-dC)5Â Îỳg/mL25Â ÎỳLTotaln/a5Â mLKeep on ice until use.SELEX wash buffer (for N samples)ReagentFinal concentrationAmountSELEX Buffern/aNÂ ÃÂ 6Â mL1Â M DTT0.5Â mMNÂ ÃÂ 3Â ÎỳLTotaln/an/aKeep on ice until use.2.Equilibrate Ni Sepharose 6 Fast Flow beads in SELEX binding buffer.a.Take out the required amount of Ni Sepharose 6 Fast Flow resin (55Â ÎỳLÂ ÃÂ number of samples) and transfer into a in a 1.5Â mL tube (e.g., for 6 samples, take out 330Â ÎỳL of Ni Sepharose 6 Fast Flow resin).Note: The total amount of resin includes a 10% excess to account for small inaccuracies when pipetting multiple samples.Note: If a large volume of Ni Sepharose 6 Fast Flow resin is required, split into several 1.5Â mL tubes (maximum 500Â ÎỳL resin/tube) and prepare additional SELEX binding buffer accordingly.b.Add 1Â mL of SELEX binding buffer and resuspend beads thoroughly by inverting the tube multiple times.c.Centrifuge for 1Â min at 400Â ÃÂ g. Discard the supernatant without disturbing the beads pellet.d.Wash beads 2Ã more times (steps 2b-c).e.Resuspend beads in SELEX binding buffer in the initial volume of resin pipetted in step a (e.g., for 6 samples, resuspend in a total volume of 330Â ÎỳL). Keep on ice until use.3.Incubate DNA-binding proteins with SELEX libraries.a.Set up SELEX reactions in 1.5Â mL tubes:SELEX reactionReagentFinal concentrationAmountHistidine-tagged DNA-binding protein10Â Îỳg/mL1Â ÎỳgSELEX DNA library (cycle N-1)1Â Îỳg/mL (15Â Îỳg/mL for the first cycle)200Â ng (1.5Â Îỳg for the first cycle)SELEX binding buffern/aup to 100Â ÎỳLTotaln/a100Â ÎỳLNote: For the first SELEX cycle, use 1.5Â Îỳg of âcycle 0â random library (see generation of cycle 0 libraries). For subsequent cycles, use 200Â ng of SELEX library from the previous cycle (e.g., To perform SELEX cycle 2, use library amplified at the end of cycle 1).Note: It is important to include a negative control SELEX reaction, without addition of proteins, to control for any sequence bias that could be associated with repeated PCR cycling. It is also advised to perform SELEX with independent libraries, which are used as technical replicates (see generation of cycle 0 libraries).e.g., Sample 1: SALL4 ZFC4Â + library 1 (replicate 1).Â Sample 2: SALL4 ZFC4Â + library 2 (replicate 2).Â Sample 3: SALL4 ZFC4Â + library 3 (replicate 3).Â Sample 4: Negative control (no protein)Â + library 1 (replicate 1).Â Sample 5: Negative control (no protein)Â + library 2 (replicate 2).Â Sample 6: Negative control (no protein)Â + library 3 (replicate 3).b.Incubate on a rotating wheel for 10Â min at room temperature.4.Purify protein-DNA complexes.a.To capture protein-DNA complexes, add 50Â ÎỳL of equilibrated Ni Sepharose 6 Fast Flow resin (from step 2) to each SELEX sample.b.Incubate for 20Â min on a rotating wheel at room temperature.c.To remove non-specifically bound DNA-protein complexes, add 1Â mL of SELEX wash buffer and resuspend beads thoroughly by inverting the tube multiple times.d.Centrifuge for 1Â min at 400Â ÃÂ g. Discard the supernatant without disturbing the beads pellet.e.Wash beads 4Ã more times (steps 4c-d).f.Resuspend the resin in 100Â ÎỳL H2O.Note: Elution of DNA from the beads is not necessary, as this material can be directly used as a template for PCR amplification of SELEX libraries.Pause point: The resin (protein-DNA complexes) can be stored at â20ÂḞC (long term). This material can be used at a later time for PCR amplification.5.PCR-amplify enriched DNA.CRITICAL: The amount of DNA bound to the resin is unknown and usually varies between SELEX samples. Therefore, it is important to empirically determine the optimal number of PCR cycles to amplify each SELEX library (see the following steps).a.For each SELEX sample, prepare a PCR mastermix (for 4Ã PCR reactions) in a 1.5Â mL tube:PCR reaction master mixReagentAmountProtein-DNA complexes (bead suspension)22.5Â ÎỳL5Ã Phusion HF Buffer45Â ÎỳLdNTPs (10Â mM)4.5Â ÎỳLLibrary FW (10Â ÎỳM)11.25Â ÎỳLLibrary RV (10Â ÎỳM)11.25Â ÎỳLPhusion DNA Polymerase2.25Â ÎỳLNuclease-free water128.25Â ÎỳLTotal225Â ÎỳLNote: It is not recommended to increase the amount of resin (DNA template) in the mix, as an excess can inhibit the PCR reaction.b.Divide mastermix between 4 PCR tubes (50Â ÎỳL/tube).Note: Before transferring the mix to PCR tubes, ensure that beads are homogeneously resuspended by pipetting up and down multiple times.c.Run each of the 4Ã PCR reactions with a different PCR programme (increasing numbers of PCR cycles):PCR cycling conditions (8Ã cycles)StepsTemperatureTimeCyclesInitial Denaturation98ÂḞC1Â min1Denaturation98ÂḞC20 s8 cyclesAnnealing68ÂḞC20 sExtension72ÂḞC20 sFinal extension72ÂḞC5Â min1Hold4ÂḞCforeverPCR cycling conditions (12Ã cycles)StepsTemperatureTimeCyclesInitial Denaturation98ÂḞC1Â min1Denaturation98ÂḞC20 s12 cyclesAnnealing68ÂḞC20 sExtension72ÂḞC20 sFinal extension72ÂḞC5Â min1Hold4ÂḞCforeverPCR cycling conditions (15Ã cycles)StepsTemperatureTimeCyclesInitial Denaturation98ÂḞC1Â min1Denaturation98ÂḞC20 s15 cyclesAnnealing68ÂḞC20 sExtension72ÂḞC20 sFinal extension72ÂḞC5Â min1Hold4ÂḞCforeverPCR cycling conditions (20Ã cycles)StepsTemperatureTimeCyclesInitial Denaturation98ÂḞC1Â min1Denaturation98ÂḞC20 s20 cyclesAnnealing68ÂḞC20 sExtension72ÂḞC20 sFinal extension72ÂḞC5Â min1Hold4ÂḞCforeverd.To control the amplification of libraries, run a small amount of PCR reaction (5Â ÎỳL) on a 10% polyacrylamide gel and stain with a 0.5Â Îỳg/mL ethidium bromide solution (see FigureÂ 3).FigureÂ 310% polyacrylamide gel showing the optimization of PCR conditions to amplify enriched DNA from Ni Sepharose beads following a cycle of SELEXFor this sample, we purified the product following to 12Ã PCR cycles, as it showed a robust amplification of the library (83Â bp) without detectable heteroduplexes (see troubleshooting 1).e.For each SELEX sample, select the optimal PCR reaction and discard other tubes (see FigureÂ 3).f.Purify DNA using the Qiagen MinElute PCR purification kit and following manufacturerâs protocol. Elute with 20Â ÎỳL of EB Buffer (included in the kit, 10Â mM Tris-HCl pH8.5) or H2O.Note: A single PCR reaction will yield enough DNA to proceed with the protocol.g.Evaluate DNA concentration and integrity of purified SELEX libraries using a Nanodrop spectrophotometer.Pause point: Store purified SELEX libraries at â20ÂḞC (long term).h.Use DNA as an input to repeat an additional cycle of SELEX (N+1).CRITICAL: Remember to save an aliquot of purified SELEX library (â20Â ng) for high-throughput sequencing (see generation of HT-SELEX libraries for Illumina sequencing).

steps.title: Bioinformatic analysis
steps.instruction:
Timing: 1Â day
Note: Analysis time will vary depending on the sequencing depth of HT-SELEX datasets and the length of DNA motifs (k-mers) to analyze.
1.Setup the package management system âcondaâ following the instructions available here: https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/user-guide/install/index.html.2.Install all required software inside a conda environment from your command line: > conda create -n eme_selex -c bioconda flexbar snakemake pip jupyterlab tqdm pandas seaborn > conda activate eme_selex > pip install eme_selex logomaker upsetplot > conda install -c plotly plotly==5.6.0 3.Generate a tab-separated values (TSV) file containing metadata of your HT-SELEX samples using the following format: Samplename libraryÂ proteinÂ cycle RV##Â lib#Â None 0 RV##Â lib#Â None 0 RV##Â lib#Â None 0 RV##Â lib#Â ZFC4 6 RV##Â lib#Â ZFC4 6 RV##Â lib#Â ZFC4 6 4.Pre-process and quality-trim sequencing reads.CRITICAL: Trim sequencing reads to the exact size of the library insert (in our case 20Â bp). For more information, regarding library design, see materials and equipment section.a.Execute flexbar for each individual sample using the following parameters:> flexbar --reads {input} --post-trim-length 20 --min-read-length 20 --qtrim-threshold 30 --output-reads {output} âfasta-output--number-tags --stdout-logÂ >Â {log}Note: Use a workflow manager such as Snakemake (https://snakemake.readthedocs.io) to automate this step for all samples.
5.Calculate k-mer frequency using the Python package eme_selex (tested on python version 3.10).a.Calculate the abundance of 5-mer motifs for all samples using the following Python code:from collections import defaultdictfrom eme_selex.eme import kmer_fraction_from_file as kfcounts, fractions, modelsÂ = defaultdict(dict), defaultdict(dict), \ defaultdict(dict)samplesÂ = [f"RV{s:02d}" for s in range(1, 22)]kÂ = 5for sample in df["SampleName"].values:Â c, f, mÂ = kf(f"fasta/{sample}.fasta.gz", k=k)Â counts[sample]Â = cÂ fractions[sample]Â = fÂ models[sample]Â = mNote: The choice of k-mers length (up to 10Â bp, see limitations) depends on the DNA-binding protein of interest. In our case, we determined that SALL4 binds to short DNA motifs of 3â5Â bp (Pantier etÂ al., 2021).b.Normalize the data and generate a data frame containing fold-change (vs cycle 0) values.Â Please refer to our bioinformatic workflow documentation (https://eme-selex.readthedocs.io) for source code.6.Visualize the enrichment of DNA motifs following HT-SELEX.
Note: To observe DNA binding of ZFC4 according to DNA base composition, we divided all 5-mer motifs into different categories depending on their proportion of A/T nucleotides (see FigureÂ 7). Please refer to our bioinformatic workflow documentation (https://eme-selex.readthedocs.io) for source code.
CRITICAL: Always compare the enrichment of k-mers (DNA motifs) with the initial random library (cycle 0) and negative control (see FigureÂ 7). These controls will confirm the specific enrichment of DNA motifs during the SELEX protocol.
Note: Here, we observed a progressive enrichment of a large number of AT-rich k-mers throughout the SELEX protocol (cycles 1/3/6), which confirmed promiscuous binding of SALL4 ZFC4. In the case of specific DNA binding, only few DNA motifs would have been enriched, with high similarity to the most abundant k-mer (Jolma etÂ al., 2010).

steps.title: Bioinformatic analysis
steps.instruction: FigureÂ 7Enrichment of all possible 5-mer DNA motifs during the HT-SELEX protocol for SALL4 ZFC4 (blue) compared to negative control (gray)DNA motifs (k-mers) were divided into six categories of increasing A/T content. Error bars indicate the technical variability with independent SELEX libraries.
